text: Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay Cowgill and Pakzad-Hurson January 15, 2025
Pay “fairness” is a big issue 2014 Gallup poll: “Fair” pay top issue facing working women in America
Pay “fairness” is a big issue 2014 Gallup poll: “Fair” pay top issue facing working women in America But what does “fairness” mean?
Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay
Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay New York DoL: “unlawful for an employer to pay an employee less than an employee of the opposite sex for equal work”

Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay New York DoL: “unlawful for an employer to pay an employee less than an employee of the opposite sex for equal work” Black proverb: “you have to be twice as good to go half as far as a white person”

Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay New York DoL: “unlawful for an employer to pay an employee less than an employee of the opposite sex for equal work” Black proverb: “you have to be twice as good to go half as far as a white person”– Violates Becker’s outcome test!

Two features about these fairness notions 1 At an individual level Most of literature on equal pay practices studies group-level consequences, not individual-level But if Ann must be paid same as Bill and Ann must be paid same as Charlie, then transitivity ⇒Bill must be paid same as Charlie.
So equal pay constraint applies to all pairs of workers, and e.g. firm cannot skirt law by paying men and women same on average but paying individuals differently

Two features about these fairness notions 1 At an individual level Most of literature on equal pay practices studies group-level consequences, not individual-level But if Ann must be paid same as Bill and Ann must be paid same as Charlie, then transitivity ⇒Bill must be paid same as Charlie.

So equal pay constraint applies to all pairs of workers, and e.g. firm cannot skirt law by paying men and women same on average but paying individuals differently 2 Complete information standard of proof (i.e. firm cannot skirt law by saying it isn’t sure if workers are equally productive or not) Laws explicitly require firms to have a uni-dimensional measure of productivity
What we do 1 Theoretically: model effects/restrictions of pay “fairness” Show various fairness notions boil down to a simple monotonicity condition Show that this condition is restrictive.
May prohibit wage bargaining, matching outside offers. 2 Empirically (earliest stages): do firms abide by monotonicity?
Study US academics, where wages publicly available for some universities and productivity (research) is observable Further avenues: effect of job transition?
Effect of new equal pay law enactment?
Model and fairness notions
Setup Single firm employing N employees {1,., N} Each worker i has productivity ∈[0, 1]
(weak) Equal Pay for Equal Work Definition The firm exhibits weak Equal Pay for Equal Work (wEPEW) if for any two workers i and j with

(weak) Equal Work for Equal Pay Definition The firm exhibits weak Equal Work for Equal Pay (wEWEP) if for any two workers i and j with wi > wj it is the case that ’s outcome test Has an incentive basis also: Suppose there is a violation so wi > wj but
Equal Pay for Equal Work Definition The firm exhibits Equal Pay for Equal Work (EPEW) if for any two workers i and j: 1 if

Equal Pay for Equal Work Definition The firm exhibits Equal Pay for Equal Work (EPEW) if for any two workers i and j: 1 if “equal pay for equal or greater work” Case law suggests this may be the right interpretation of EPEW laws
Putting it all together Proposition The following conditions are all equivalent: 1 wEPEW + wEWEP, 2 EPEW, 3 there exists a non-decreasing function

Putting it all together Proposition The following conditions are all equivalent: 1 wEPEW + wEWEP, 2 EPEW, 3 there exists a non-decreasing function “strong” version of EWEP, which if placed in result above changes “non-decreasing” to “strictly increasing”

Aside: Other fairness notions?
CEO should not make more than X times what workers make Equal pay for “similar” work Equal pay for all These all add upper bound to slope of wage function
Monotonicity restrictive, unintended consequences?

RI Pay Equity Act: “An employer may, for the limited purpose of ‘support[ing] a wage higher than the wage [initially] offered by the employer,’ consider and seek to confirm a job applicant’s wage history if such wage history was voluntarily provided.” Hall and Krueger: 1/3 of workers bargained wage before starting Matching outside offers
Monotonicity restrictive, unintended consequences?

RI Pay Equity Act: “An employer may, for the limited purpose of ‘support[ing] a wage higher than the wage [initially] offered by the employer,’ consider and seek to confirm a job applicant’s wage history if such wage history was voluntarily provided.” Hall and Krueger: 1/3 of workers bargained wage before starting Matching outside offers Proposition Consider a firm that abides by monotonicity.
Fix ε > 0, and increase a (uniformly) random worker’s wage by →1.
Questions Do firms abide by monotonicity?
Does exogenous shock (enactment of new EPEW legislation) make firms more likely to abide by monotonicity?
Do new hires (potential bargaining) disrupt monotonicity?
Questions Do firms abide by monotonicity?
Does exogenous shock (enactment of new EPEW legislation) make firms more likely to abide by monotonicity?
Do new hires (potential bargaining) disrupt monotonicity?
To answer these questions we need a setting where wages and productivity are observable.

Setting Study tenure-track and tenured faculty at US research universities Pay is publicly observable for most state universities by law Research output observable for faculty, plausibly captures most of a faculty member’s productivity
Our Data Individual −2019.
Sources: Publication & citation records: Card, DellaVigna, Funk & Iriberri.
Salary & personal details: State universities, FOIA requests by media.
Key individual covariates: University/department & gender.

Example: Mascal Pichaillat moves Brown →UCSC in 2022 Observe: man In year 2021 observe: 1 QJE publication in 2015 with citation counts 16,14,., in years 2015,2016,.,2021, respectively Works in unknown economics department Rank is unknown Salary is unknown In 2022 observe: QJE publication also has 20 citations in 2020 Works in UCSC economics department Rank is associate professor Salary is X In 2023 observe: QJE publication also has 12 citations in 2023 Works in UCSC economics department Rank is now full professor Salary is Y

Estimating Researcher Productivity We have two broad approaches. 1) Use raw publication, journals & citation counts as several different measures of productivity (without combining them). 2) Combine publications, journal, and citations data into a single omnibus measure based.
We present two possible “omnibus methods” next.
Goal: Proof of concept of ideas in theory section.
We likely cannot perfectly estimate “productivity.”
Omnibus Method #1: Pope & Snydor-like approach Based on: Pope & Snydor Estimate regression of salary as a function of observables, in particular: Research productivity observables (pubs +cites).
Demographic variable (gender).
To estimate a researcher’s productivity, use coefficients as if everyone were the same gender.
Necessary to include gender in the regression to make sure other coefficients are set correctly.
Now we have set of {wage, imputed productivity} pairs.
Omnibus Method #1: Where do we go from here?
Fit isotonic regression to department-year-rank(?) cells.
MSE > 0 within cell ⇒failure of monotonicity.
Does MSE change around enactment of new equal pay?
California enacts new equal pay law in 2016, how does this affect UCSC. (Possibly related to above) Do new arrivals/departures upturn/reset the monotonicity apple cart?
Omnibus Method #2: Rationalizing Salaries Inspiration: Rambachan.

Paper asks: “do (linear) preferences exist that rationalize observed choices without prediction mistakes?” Our version: Is there a (linear) weighting of publications at different journals that would rationalize observed salaries as monotonic?
Omnibus Method #2: Rationalizing Salaries Linear preferences: A university’s wage function as a linear combination of K research productivity metrics.

Omnibus Method #2: Rationalizing Salaries Solution to this LP is either 1) Empty set: Wages cannot be rationalized as monotonic. 2) Feasible region: Wages can be rationalized as monotonic, but only if the βs weights lie inside the region, aka the “monotonicity zone.” Convex polyhedron (or unbounded).
Do actual weights βs fall within this region?
If it is empty, then no.
Idea: Estimate Equation 1 as a regression, i.e.
